text: perineal staple rectal resection for a full thickness, very large rectal prolapse in a frail elderly patient. Here we present a ninety year old female patient with a past medical history of atrial fibrillation, history of primary excision for squamous cell carcinoma from the lower leg in two thousand seventeen, local recurrence exercise in two thousand eighteen and further lymphadenectomy for recurrence in the left groin in two thousand eighteen. The patient presented with a full thickness rectal prolapse measuring ten centimeters which was having a significant negative impact on her quality of life.

She was offered a perineal stapled prolapse resection, which is a relatively novel surgical procedure for treating external rectal prolapse with good clinical outcome. Frail patients who are unsuitable for prolonged abdominal procedures and can only endure a short procedure under regional anesthesia should be considered for perineal stapled rectal prolapse resection. PSPR is also beneficial for elderly patients with a large rectal prolapse, approximately eight to ten centimeters, and multiple comorbidities. In addition, PSPR has shown equally good clinical outcomes with young patients who want to avoid potential complication of nerve damage that is associated with the abdominal approach.
The patient is positioned in lithotomy with a slight Trendelenburg tilt. The surgeon reproduces the rectal prolapse by grasping the apex of the prolapsed tissue.

The surgeon resects the prolapsed rectum longitudinally at the three o'clock position using a reloadable 100mm GIA linear stapler. The stapler is held in place for at least one minute before firing due to the thickness and edema of the tissues.
The surgeon repeats the resection step at the nine o'clock position, resulting in the formation of anterior and posterior prolapsed rectal flaps.
The surgeon resects the anterior rectal flap horizontally at the anorectal junction, ensuring the resection is parallel to and avoids the dentate line.
The surgeon uses a reloadable endo-GIA linear stapling device to resect the posterior rectal flap, demonstrating the device's flexibility and ability to be angled during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a contour stapler for precise transection of the rectal prolapse. After the contour stapler is disengaged, the anal mucosa and neorectum spontaneously fall back into place, indicating the completion of the resection.
